title: Investors Elevator Pitch · Q&A Playbook
subtitle: Nine answers. One thesis. One platform that finally tells biology's actual story.

sections.label: Problems Addressed
sections.subtitle: Five expensive, interlocking failures
sections.headline: What are the problems we are addressing — name five.
sections.intro: Each one BiRAGAS was built to break.

bullets.body: Ninety percent of drugs that enter clinical trials fail. The dirty secret: most failures aren't random — they're predictable. The wrong target was chosen at the very beginning, costing the sponsor on average two billion dollars and a decade of work per failed program.

The entire biomedical research infrastructure — genomics platforms, sequencing tools, expression profiling — generates association data. It tells you Gene A and Gene B move together in disease. It cannot tell you which one is the driver and which is a passenger. A drug aimed at a passenger never works.

FDA guidance has shifted: regulators now explicitly require mechanistic, causal evidence to support therapeutic claims. Most discovery platforms were never built to produce that evidence and cannot retrofit it.

Today's omics tools require a trained bioinformatician to drive them. That throttles the addressable user from every translational scientist down to the few who can run a pipeline. Drug-development velocity is throttled by tooling, not biology.

Discovery teams stitch together FASTQ aligners, DEG callers, pathway tools, and CRISPR analytics from different vendors. No shared provenance, no auditable chain, no FDA-submittable dossier — every handoff is a place where evidence dies.

sections.label: Major Competitors
sections.subtitle: Named, with positioning
sections.headline: What our major competitors have done or are doing — named.
sections.intro: The AI-drug-discovery field is crowded, but it is crowded downstream of the problem BiRAGAS solves.

bullets.body: Phenomic screening + ML-driven molecular design. Operates downstream of target ID. Productive on optimization, silent on causal target validation.
Generative chemistry and molecule design. Builds new molecules for already-chosen targets. Beautiful tooling, but presumes the target is right.
Physics-based molecular simulation and lead optimization. The gold standard for downstream chemistry. Same blind spot upstream.

Knowledge-graph drug repurposing. Associative reasoning over biomedical literature — closer to BiRAGAS conceptually, but no mechanistic validation layer, no Mendelian randomization, no in-silico perturbation.
Clinical genomics correlation analytics at scale. Excellent at sequencing throughput; structurally a correlation engine.
Molecular profiling and companion diagnostics. Tier-one infrastructure player, but profiling-first, not causation-first.
General-purpose AI labs running biomedical experiments on association prediction. No regulatory architecture, no causal validation, no FDA-grade evidence dossier.

sections.footnote: Top-five players collectively held only 11.8% of the AI drug-discovery market in 2025. No dominant platform exists yet. The space is wide open at the layer BiRAGAS occupies.

sections.label: How We Resolve
sections.subtitle: Five operational answers, shipping today
sections.headline: How are we going to resolve these — name five.

bullets.lead: ① Four-stream convergence on one causal graph.
bullets.body: Twenty-five modules, eight causal engines, three stacked tiers. T1 — Causal / Drug Target stack (causal calculus, DAG builder, GWAS/Mendelian randomization, in-silico perturbation, SIGNOR causality, centrality). T2 — Core analysis (DEG, GSEA, multi-omics, single-cell, temporal Granger, total RNA pipeline). T3 — Data and processing (FASTQ, harmonization, CRISPR Perturb-seq, screening, amplicon). One reasoning engine, end-to-end.
Every output is ranked: Tier 1 Driver / Tier 2 Strong Candidate / Tier 3 Candidate. No black boxes. Source-linked at every step. Built to look exactly like the evidence an FDA reviewer expects.
Every output ships with a regulatory-grade evidence dossier. The architecture isn't bolted on — it is the architecture. FDA alignment as a product feature, not a service.

Natural-language agent that sits in front of all 25 modules. A scientist types a question — find DEGs, map causal edges for these genes, run temporal Granger — and Causion classifies intent, routes to the correct module, configures parameters, and returns the result. Bioinformatician no longer required.

FASTQ to FDA-ready causal target dossier in one engine. T3 ingests and harmonizes. T2 runs DEG, pathway, prioritization. T1 builds the causal DAG, validates with Mendelian randomization, perturbs in silico, and triages targets. Every step auditable. No handoffs.

sections.label: Why We Win
sections.subtitle: Better than the named competitors
sections.headline: What makes us better than our competitors.
sections.intro: BiRAGAS is not fighting Recursion, Insilico, or Schrödinger for the same square of the board. It operates one square upstream — and that square is the one the FDA increasingly demands.

bullets.body: All named competitors operate at molecular design and optimization. BiRAGAS operates at causal target identification and validation — the highest-stakes step in the entire pipeline. Get this wrong and nothing downstream matters.

Tempus, Foundation, and most genomics platforms produce association data. BiRAGAS produces causal claims, validated through MR, perturbation, and convergence across four independent biological streams.
Causal Confidence Scoring + auto-generated dossiers structured for FDA submission. Competitors must build this layer; we ship it.

BiRAGAS is founded and led by Dr. Mohamad Ayass — a physician-scientist who built the platform from clinical practice, understanding what physicians actually need from molecular evidence. That domain depth cannot be manufactured by a generalist AI lab.

Twenty-five modules in one reasoning engine versus a stack of point tools. Once a partner integrates BiRAGAS into their workflow and their regulatory filings, the switching cost is the dossier itself.
Recursion, Insilico, and Schrödinger need a causal target layer they don't have. Long-term, the most likely outcome is partnership or acquisition, not direct competition.

sections.label: Who We Sell To
sections.subtitle: Three tiers of customer
sections.headline: Who are we going to sell this to.
sections.intro: Each tier entered through a different module, each compounding into the next.

bullets.body: Full BiRAGAS platform license, enterprise-wide. 270+ pharma and biotech companies now run active AI drug-discovery programs (up from fewer than 80 in 2020). Each one is a candidate.
Modular per-program licensing — start with one Causal Engine on a single program, expand to multi-omics and temporal, deepen into T1 causal validation as targets advance.
Project-based access. Lower revenue per account but powerful for distribution, publication, and grant-funded pipelines.
Top-20 pharma co-development of first-in-class companion diagnostics (FLT3 / IDH / menin AML; autoimmune biologic responder). Upfront + milestones + per-test royalty.
CPRIT, NIH, DOD, and academic-medical-center grants. Pipeline value $1.5M to $10M per program, non-dilutive.
Service contracts and consulting against the BiRAGAS engine.
Track A — pharma service contracts $500K–$2M. Track B — platform licensing $300K–$3M. Track C — investor SAFE notes $500K–$20M.
Modular SaaS-adjacent licensing across pharma, biotech, and AMCs.
Top-20 pharma upfront + milestones + per-test royalty.
Asset-participation interests in the BiRAGAS software asset (separate from company equity).
Cross-vertical revenue mapping spanning AI drug discovery, AML, DKD, wound care / AMR, pancreatic, melanoma, wellness, autoimmune, Alzheimer, and agentic AI verticals.

sections.label: External Validation
sections.subtitle: Partners and validators already in place
sections.headline: Partners providing external validation.
sections.intro: BiRAGAS does not validate itself by claim. It validates itself by association — already.

bullets.body: USC, UAMS, Harvard (×2), UC Irvine (×2). Active research collaborations spanning AML, diabetic nephropathy, melanoma, and computational pathology.

Dr. Bryan Reynolds (UCI, Neuro-Cognitive), Dr. Amal Alachkar (UCI, Neuro-Cognitive), Dr. Houda Alachkar (USC, AML), Dr. John Imig (UAMS, diabetic nephropathy), Dr. Andrzej Krolewski (Joslin / Harvard, DKD), Dr. John Kirkwood (UPMC, melanoma).
Peer-reviewed publications across transcriptomics, aptamer science, and case reports of treatment decisions guided by the BiRAGAS platform.

sections.label: Money & Returns
sections.subtitle: How much, by when, what investors make
sections.headline: How will we make money — how much, by when, and how much do investors stand to make.
sections.intro: The structure is built for a fast, accountable re-rate. Capital funds three discrete catalysts before the institutional Series A prices.

bullets.body: SAFE bridge round. Y Combinator post-money form. 20% discount. No valuation cap. Rolling close. 1.25× share advantage layered on top of the discount.
Two senior pharma BD, one CCO, two field engineers, one reimbursement lead. Re-rating trigger: three pharma anchors signed plus first LDT commercial launch.
Two AMC master service agreements. Bio-RAG corpus expansion from 8.6M to 15M biological vectors. RWE access via two payer-aligned health systems.
Top-20 pharma co-development for FLT3 / IDH / menin AML or autoimmune biologic-responder. Upfront + milestones + per-test royalty.

Today: ~$60M post-money reference. After Catalyst ①: $110–140M (+1.5–2.5× platform multiple). After Catalyst ②: $150–180M (+0.5–1.0× incremental). After Catalyst ③: $350–500M (+2–3× commercial inflection).
8% non-compounded preferred return on the BiRAGAS PPM, then 85/15 split (Holder/Manager) on Net BiRAGAS Revenue.
A SAFE today converts against a ~5× re-rate, compounded by the 20% discount and 1.25× share advantage, in a 12-month execution window.
Sale, exclusive license, or spin-out of the BiRAGAS Asset triggers waterfall. At Year-5 base-case $287M ARR, valuation multiples on biotech infrastructure SaaS imply a $3B+ outcome envelope.

sections.label: Slam-Dunk Thesis
sections.subtitle: Eight categories of de-risking
sections.headline: What makes us a no-brainer, slam-dunk investment.
sections.intro: Eight reasons — each a different category of de-risking.

bullets.body: Twenty-five modules. Eight causal engines. The technology exists, runs, and produces source-linked outputs. The bet is on commercialization, not invention.
Founder Dr. Mohamad Ayass self-funded $23M of R&D over 15 years. No cap-table baggage. No early dilution. The asset comes in clean.
FDA explicitly requires mechanistic causal evidence. BiRAGAS architecturally produces it. Other platforms must retrofit.
$3.1B today, $43.9B by 2035, 30%+ CAGR. Top-five players hold only 11.8% share. The infrastructure layer is unclaimed.
Three concrete milestones, each a discrete re-rating event. Investors can verify each one before the next gate closes.
Six academic medical centers and peer-reviewed publications. Not a pitch-deck claim — an existing network.
Modular T2 entry → enterprise T1 expansion → T3 integration lock-in. Switching costs compound through the partner's regulatory filings.
BiRAGAS PPM Units are fractional beneficial interests in the BiRAGAS Software Asset, not equity in Ayass Bioscience LLC. Clean alignment, cleaner liquidity story.

manifesto: We're not building another AI that finds associations.
We're building the infrastructure that finally tells biology's actual story.
